questions.question: Define the following terms: (i) Height of a tree (ii) Depth of a node (iii) Leaf node
keyPoints: Height: Maximum depth of any node from root; Leaf: Node with no children; Depth: Number of edges from root to node

questions.question: Differentiate between: (i) Full binary tree and Complete binary tree (ii) Inorder and Preorder traversal
keyPoints: Full: 0 or 2 children each node; Complete: All levels filled except possibly last, left-aligned; Inorder: L-Root-Right; Preorder: Root-Left-Right

questions.question: Write the algorithm for inorder traversal of a binary tree.
keyPoints: Recursively: Inorder(left), visit(root), Inorder(right); Or using stack for iterative version; Produces sorted output for BST

questions.question: Explain why postorder traversal is used for deleting a tree.
keyPoints: Children must be processed before parent; Ensures memory freed from bottom to top; Prevents accessing deallocated memory; Order: Left subtree → Right subtree → Root

questions.question: What is the maximum number of nodes in a binary tree of height h? Also find height of a tree with 15 nodes.
keyPoints: Max nodes = 2^(h+1) - 1; For 15 nodes, 2^(h+1)-1=15 gives h+1=4, so h=3; Or can also be perfect tree with height 3

questions.question: Explain the difference between preorder, inorder, and postorder traversals with example.
keyPoints: Preorder (Root-L-R): Used for copying trees; Inorder (L-Root-R): Sorted output for BST; Postorder (L-R-Root): Used for deletion; Each visits root at different position

questions.question: What is a threaded binary tree? State one advantage of using it.
keyPoints: Replaces NULL pointers with threads to predecessor/successor; Allows traversal without stack or recursion; Saves space; Faster traversal

questions.question: Construct a binary tree from the following traversals: Preorder: A B D E C F G and Inorder: D B E A F C G. Show the tree structure and verify with postorder traversal.
keyPoints: Root from preorder = A; Split inorder: left=D B E, right=F C G; Preorder of left gives B (root of left subtree), D and E are children; Continue building; Final tree has A as root with left subtree (B with children D,E) and right subtree (C with children F,G); Postorder verification: D E B F G C A

questions.question: Write an algorithm for level order traversal of a binary tree. Explain how queue is used in this process. Also compute time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Use queue; Enqueue root; While queue not empty: Dequeue, visit node, Enqueue left child if exists, Enqueue right child if exists; Time: O(n) - visit each node; Space: O(w) where w is max width, O(n) worst case for complete tree; At most n+1/2 nodes in queue

questions.question: Define a Binary Search Tree (BST). Explain how search operation works in BST with an example. What is the time complexity for search in worst case and average case?
keyPoints: BST: Left subtree values < root < Right subtree values; Search: Start at root, if target < current go left, if > go right, repeat until found or NULL; Worst case O(n) for skewed tree; Average case O(log n) for balanced tree; Example searching 45 in BST with root 30, right child 50, left child 20 with right child 45

questions.question: Explain the following types of binary trees with suitable diagrams: (i) Full binary tree (ii) Complete binary tree (iii) Perfect binary tree
keyPoints: Full: Every node has 0 or 2 children; Complete: All levels filled except last, nodes left-aligned; Perfect: All internal nodes have 2 children, all leaves same level; Provide diagrams showing each type with 7 nodes as example; Height relationships differ
